meta.name: AWS Certified Machine Learning Engineer - Associate

questions.scenario: An ML team is preparing training data for a customer churn model. The raw data is 2 TB across 12 CSV files in S3, containing customer demographics, transaction history, and support tickets. Data quality is inconsistent — missing values, mixed date formats, outliers. The team is not comfortable writing Spark code but wants reproducible transformations that can be reused in production inference pipelines.
questions.question: Which data preparation approach BEST fits the team's constraints?
choices: AWS Glue ETL jobs written in PySpark, with Glue Data Catalog for metadata.
Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler to visually build transformation flows, then export as a SageMaker Processing job or Feature Store definition for reuse in production pipelines.
Manual cleanup in Athena SQL queries, results written back to S3.
Import the CSVs into Amazon Redshift and use SQL for cleanup.
questions.explanation:
Data Wrangler provides a visual interface for data exploration and transformation (handling missing values, outliers, date formatting, feature engineering) with 300+ built-in transforms. Flows export as SageMaker Processing jobs, Feature Store definitions, or Python code — giving you reproducibility and production reuse without requiring Spark expertise.

Glue PySpark (A) requires Spark skills the team doesn't have. Athena SQL (C) works for some transformations but isn't reproducible as an ML pipeline artifact. Redshift (D) adds a data warehouse layer the use case doesn't need.
questions.pattern: 'Visual data prep' + 'non-coders' + 'reusable in production' → Data Wrangler. It's AWS's answer to KNIME/Dataiku for teams without Spark expertise. Key trigger phrases: 'visual,' 'without code,' 'exploratory prep,' 'export to training pipeline.'

references.title: Build ML data pipelines with Data Wrangler

questions.scenario: A team is training a binary classification model for fraud detection. The dataset is highly imbalanced: 0.3% of transactions are fraudulent. Initial XGBoost training with default parameters produces 99.7% accuracy but catches only 12% of fraud cases. Business cares more about catching fraud (recall) than avoiding false positives, within reason — the fraud team can review ~50 flagged transactions per hour across the company.
questions.question: Which combination of techniques BEST improves the model for this business constraint?
choices: Keep accuracy as the primary metric; increase training epochs and model complexity.

Switch to recall as the primary metric, apply SMOTE to oversample the minority class, tune XGBoost's scale_pos_weight hyperparameter, and use precision-recall curves to choose a decision threshold that respects the 50/hr review capacity.
Remove the imbalance by undersampling the majority class to 1:1 with the minority class.
Use accuracy but add more features to the model.
questions.explanation:
For imbalanced classification, accuracy is misleading — a model predicting 'not fraud' for everything achieves 99.7%. Techniques that matter: SMOTE for synthetic minority oversampling, XGBoost's scale_pos_weight parameter to weight the minority class, and PR curves (not ROC) to choose a threshold. The review capacity constraint maps to precision at a specific recall target.

More epochs (A) doesn't fix imbalance. Undersampling to 1:1 (C) throws away 99.7% of training data. More features (D) doesn't address the imbalance problem.
questions.pattern: Imbalanced classification patterns: stop using accuracy → use F1, recall, AUPRC. Techniques: SMOTE, class weights (scale_pos_weight in XGBoost), threshold tuning. Review-capacity-constrained problems map to 'what precision at X% recall' trade-offs.

references.title: Handling imbalanced datasets in AWS ML

questions.scenario: An ML platform team supports 30 data scientists building models that use overlapping features (customer aggregates, transaction statistics, product embeddings). Each DS currently computes features independently in their notebooks, leading to inconsistent definitions and duplicate compute. The team wants a central way to define, compute, store, and serve features for both training and online inference with low latency.
questions.question: Which service BEST addresses this?
choices: S3 with Parquet files and a shared naming convention; Athena for training queries and DynamoDB for online lookup.

Amazon SageMaker Feature Store — provides online (low-latency key-value) and offline (historical S3-backed) stores with consistent feature definitions. Integrates with SageMaker Processing for feature computation and training jobs.
A custom solution using Redis for online features and Redshift for training features.
Amazon DynamoDB with GSIs for all features, shared across training and inference.
questions.explanation:
SageMaker Feature Store is AWS's managed feature store: online store (DynamoDB-backed) for low-latency inference lookup, offline store (S3-backed, queryable via Athena) for training, with feature groups providing consistent definitions and versioning. Addresses every requirement: central definitions, dual-serve, low-latency online.

Custom S3 + DynamoDB (A) rebuilds what Feature Store provides. Redis + Redshift (C) is unmanaged infrastructure. DynamoDB-only (D) lacks historical storage for training.
questions.pattern: 'Central feature definitions' + 'training and online inference' → SageMaker Feature Store. Key concepts: online store (ms latency), offline store (training), feature groups, time-travel queries. This is a flagship MLA topic.

questions.scenario: An ML engineer deploys a real-time SageMaker endpoint for credit risk scoring. Compliance requires: no training data or inference inputs leave the VPC, encryption at rest and in transit with customer-managed KMS keys, all API calls audited, and the model artifacts protected from unauthorized access. The endpoint must be reachable from application servers in the same VPC but not from the internet.
questions.question: Which configuration BEST meets these requirements?
choices: Deploy the endpoint to the default VPC with IAM policies restricting access. Use AWS-managed KMS keys.

Deploy the SageMaker endpoint in VPC-only mode (no internet). Use VPC interface endpoints (PrivateLink) for SageMaker Runtime, S3, and other required services. Customer-managed KMS keys for S3 artifacts and endpoint storage. CloudTrail data events for inference API logging. S3 bucket policies restricting model artifact access.
Deploy with a public endpoint but use Security Groups to restrict source IPs.
Deploy on EC2 with custom inference code and use VPC flow logs for auditing.
questions.explanation:
This is the canonical 'locked-down ML' configuration. SageMaker endpoints support VPC-only mode with no internet access, using VPC interface endpoints for SageMaker API/Runtime and any other AWS services. Customer-managed KMS keys provide key control. CloudTrail data events capture inference calls (API calls themselves are always logged; data events cover the runtime invocations). S3 bucket policies protect artifacts.

Default VPC + managed KMS (A) doesn't meet 'customer-managed KMS' or VPC restriction. Public endpoint (C) doesn't meet no-internet. EC2 custom (D) abandons the managed service benefits.
questions.pattern: ML security stack: VPC-only mode (no internet), VPC endpoints (private service access), customer-managed KMS (key sovereignty), CloudTrail data events (runtime audit), bucket policies (artifact protection). These appear together when 'regulated,' 'locked down,' or 'no internet' is in the scenario.

references.title: SageMaker encryption at rest and in transit

questions.scenario: A team deploys an ML model behind a SageMaker real-time endpoint serving 500 requests/second at ~40ms p99 latency. They want to release v2 of the model. The business cannot accept any downtime and wants to validate v2 on a small fraction of traffic before full rollout. If v2 performs poorly, they need fast rollback.
questions.question: Which deployment strategy BEST fits these requirements?
choices: Replace the endpoint in-place by calling UpdateEndpoint with the new model.

Use SageMaker endpoint multi-variant deployment (Production Variants) to host v1 and v2 behind the same endpoint, sending 90/10 traffic split via variant weights. Monitor v2 metrics, gradually shift traffic, roll back by adjusting weights if needed.
Deploy v2 to a separate endpoint; use Route 53 weighted routing to split traffic.
Deploy v2 in shadow mode with SageMaker Shadow Tests, then switch entirely to v2.
questions.explanation:
Production variants let you host multiple model versions behind a single endpoint with traffic-split control via variant weights. Traffic shifts and rollback are simple weight updates. This is the AWS-canonical canary/blue-green pattern for ML endpoints.

In-place update (A) has brief downtime / risk during switchover. Route 53 weighted (C) adds DNS resolution latency and an extra moving part. Shadow tests (D) are for validating inference without affecting users — doesn't gradually shift production traffic.
questions.pattern: SageMaker deployment patterns: Production Variants = canary/blue-green with weight split. Shadow deployment = invoke v2 in parallel but don't return its response (validation only). Blue/green update = full switch with quick rollback. Canary → Production Variants + weights.

questions.scenario: A deployed recommendation model was trained on 2023 user behavior data. Over 6 months in production, conversion rates from its recommendations have steadily declined. The team suspects data drift (user preferences shifting) but needs quantitative evidence and automated alerting so they don't have to manually watch metrics.
questions.question: Which approach BEST provides drift detection and alerting?
choices: Add custom CloudWatch metrics to the application that tracks conversion rates.

Enable SageMaker Model Monitor with Data Quality and Model Quality baselines captured from training data. Schedule monitoring jobs on inference traffic. Model Monitor publishes drift metrics to CloudWatch and triggers alarms on threshold violations.
Retrain the model monthly on a schedule regardless of drift evidence.
Use AWS Glue to periodically compare training and inference data distributions.
questions.explanation:
SageMaker Model Monitor is purpose-built for this. It captures a baseline from training data (statistics and constraints), then periodically analyzes captured inference data to detect data drift (input distribution changes), model quality drift (prediction accuracy drift — requires labels), bias drift, and feature attribution drift. Emits CloudWatch metrics, enabling automated alerting.

Custom CloudWatch metrics (A) track business metrics but not distribution drift. Blind retraining (C) wastes compute and doesn't confirm drift. Glue (D) can work but is custom code vs Model Monitor's managed approach.
questions.pattern: 'Drift detection for deployed model' → SageMaker Model Monitor. Four monitor types: Data Quality (input drift, no labels needed), Model Quality (requires labels), Bias Drift, Feature Attribution Drift. Baseline is captured from training data. Runs on a schedule.

questions.scenario: A team trains an image classification model. They have 50,000 labeled images and want to maximize model accuracy with limited compute budget. They observe overfitting — training accuracy reaches 98% but validation accuracy plateaus at 82%. The validation set is representative of production data.
questions.question: Which combination MOST effectively addresses overfitting while respecting compute budget?
choices: Increase model capacity (more layers) and train longer.

Apply data augmentation (rotation, flip, color jitter), add dropout and weight regularization, use early stopping based on validation loss, and consider transfer learning from a pre-trained model to leverage learned representations.
Collect 500,000 more labeled images to reduce overfitting.
Switch to a simpler algorithm like logistic regression.
questions.explanation:
Classic overfitting remediation: data augmentation (artificially expand dataset), regularization (dropout + weight decay), early stopping (stop before memorization), and transfer learning (use pre-trained features — often the single biggest lever for limited-data image tasks). All compute-budget-aware.

More capacity + longer training (A) worsens overfitting. 500k more labeled images (C) violates compute budget and label budget. Logistic regression (D) can't compete on image classification.
questions.pattern: Overfitting toolkit: regularization (L1/L2/dropout), data augmentation, early stopping, transfer learning, reduce model capacity. The exam tests whether you know MULTIPLE techniques — single-technique answers are often distractors.

questions.scenario: An ML team has an existing training pipeline using a Jupyter notebook that: downloads raw data from S3, preprocesses with pandas, trains with XGBoost, evaluates, and uploads model to S3. They want to: run this on a schedule, have reproducibility, track lineage of which data produced which model, support parallel hyperparameter search, and have a human approval gate before the model moves to production.
questions.question: Which AWS service BEST converts this notebook workflow into a production pipeline?
choices: AWS Step Functions with Lambda for each step.

Amazon SageMaker Pipelines with steps for Processing, Training (plus Hyperparameter Tuning step), Evaluation, Model Registry registration, and a ConditionStep or manual Model Registry approval gate. Lineage is tracked automatically.
AWS Glue workflows orchestrating the entire pipeline.
Airflow on Amazon MWAA with SageMaker operators.
questions.explanation:
SageMaker Pipelines is the purpose-built ML pipeline orchestrator, native to SageMaker: Processing steps for prep, Training step, HPT step for hyperparameter search, Evaluation steps, Model Registry for versioning and approval gates. Lineage tracking (ML metadata) is automatic. The Model Registry approval state controls whether models proceed to deployment.

Step Functions (A) can orchestrate but lacks native ML concepts like Model Registry, HPT integration, and automatic lineage. Glue (C) is ETL-focused. MWAA (D) works but is heavier weight and not ML-native.
questions.pattern: 'Production ML pipeline' + 'SageMaker-native' → SageMaker Pipelines + Model Registry. Step Functions can orchestrate but lacks ML-specific primitives. MWAA/Airflow is valid but heavier. When the scenario emphasizes lineage, HPT, and model approval, Pipelines is the answer.

questions.scenario: A team ingests streaming clickstream data (~20,000 events/sec) to build near-real-time ML features (rolling counts over 5-minute and 1-hour windows, unique visitor rates). Features must be available to an online model within 30 seconds of the original event. The offline training pipeline needs the same features computed historically from the same raw data.
questions.question: Which architecture BEST builds this dual-serve feature pipeline?
choices: Kinesis Data Streams → Lambda for aggregation → DynamoDB for online features; separate Glue job for historical features.

Kinesis Data Streams → Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink for windowed aggregations → write features to both SageMaker Feature Store online (low-latency) and offline (S3) stores, keeping training and inference feature computation consistent.
Kinesis Data Firehose → S3 → batch Glue jobs every 5 minutes.
SQS → EC2 workers → Redshift for both training and online lookup.
questions.explanation:
Flink provides windowed aggregations (5-min, 1-hour) natively with exactly-once semantics. Writing to Feature Store's dual online/offline stores keeps training and inference computation consistent — a common cause of ML bugs (training-serving skew). Features flow to the online store for 30-second inference lookup and offline store for historical training.

Lambda + separate Glue (A) causes training-serving skew. Firehose + batch Glue (C) can't meet 30-second latency. SQS + Redshift (D) lacks streaming aggregation primitives.
questions.pattern: Streaming features with training-serving consistency → Flink (for windowed agg) + Feature Store (for dual-serve). Training-serving skew is a known ML failure mode the exam tests — answers with separate training and serving feature computation are traps.

references.title: Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink

questions.scenario: A team must decide between three algorithms for a tabular classification task with 200 features and 2M rows: (1) logistic regression, (2) random forest, (3) XGBoost. They want strong performance on a task with potentially non-linear interactions but also need to explain individual predictions to business stakeholders and regulators.
questions.question: Which approach BEST balances predictive performance and explainability?
choices: Logistic regression — fully explainable via coefficients, even if accuracy is lower.

XGBoost — usually highest accuracy on tabular; use SHAP values (via SageMaker Clarify) to generate per-prediction explanations. Feature importance and SHAP collectively satisfy explainability needs without sacrificing accuracy.
Random forest — interpretable by averaging over trees.
A deep neural network — highest ceiling, use LIME for explanations.
questions.explanation:
XGBoost is typically the top performer on tabular data. Modern ML explainability doesn't require linear models — SHAP values provide mathematically sound per-prediction attributions (sum to prediction-baseline), work with any tree-based model, and are produced by SageMaker Clarify. This gets you accuracy AND regulator-acceptable explanations.

Logistic regression (A) sacrifices accuracy. Random forest (C) is less interpretable than XGBoost with SHAP and typically less accurate. DNN (D) is overkill for tabular and less explainable.
questions.pattern: 'Tabular + explainability' → XGBoost + SHAP via Clarify. The 'linear = explainable, complex = black box' framing is outdated. Clarify produces SHAP values and is the MLA-C01 exam's expected answer for model explanation.

questions.scenario: A team is training an ML model on sensitive medical data. Regulations require: (1) raw patient identifiers must never appear in training data, (2) data scientists must not have direct access to raw records, (3) audit logs showing who accessed what subset of data, (4) irreversible anonymization before the data reaches ML workspaces.
questions.question: Which pipeline BEST meets these requirements?
choices: A pre-processing pipeline using Amazon Macie to detect PII in the raw bucket. AWS Glue DataBrew or Glue ETL to apply hashing/tokenization/suppression of identifiers, writing anonymized data to a separate bucket with bucket policy limiting data scientist access. Lake Formation for fine-grained access control and audit via CloudTrail.
Give data scientists IAM access to raw data with CloudTrail logging.
Store raw data in KMS-encrypted S3; data scientists decrypt in their notebooks.
Use SageMaker Ground Truth to manually anonymize each record.
questions.explanation:
This is a data governance pipeline: Macie to discover PII, DataBrew or Glue to irreversibly transform it (hash, tokenize, suppress), bucket-level separation of raw and anonymized data, Lake Formation for fine-grained access (data scientists only see anonymized), and CloudTrail for audit. Each layer addresses a specific requirement.

Direct IAM (B) violates 'no direct access.' Encrypted storage (C) isn't anonymization — encryption is reversible. Ground Truth (D) is for labeling, not anonymization.
questions.pattern: PII/PHI governance pipeline: Macie (detection) + DataBrew/Glue (transformation) + Lake Formation (access control) + CloudTrail (audit). The exam tests whether you separate raw and anonymized zones with different access controls.

questions.scenario: A credit scoring model is audited for bias. The team must report whether the model's predictions differ across demographic groups (age, race, gender) in ways that could indicate unfair treatment. The auditor wants to see pre-training data bias (class imbalance across groups), post-training model bias (differences in model behavior), and per-prediction explanations.
questions.question: Which service and configuration MOST efficiently produces the required reports?
choices: Custom analysis in SageMaker Studio notebooks, manually computing metrics.

SageMaker Clarify running: (a) pre-training bias analysis on the dataset before training, (b) post-training bias analysis on the trained model using a labeled validation set, (c) explainability report with SHAP values. Output reports are attached to the Model Registry entry for audit.
AWS Trusted Advisor for ML bias checks.
Amazon Comprehend for demographic analysis of predictions.
questions.explanation:
SageMaker Clarify is purpose-built: pre-training bias metrics (class imbalance across groups, DPL, KL divergence, etc.), post-training bias metrics (disparate impact, difference in conditional outcomes), and SHAP-based explainability. Reports can be attached to Model Registry entries for audit trail.

Custom notebook analysis (A) is reinventing Clarify. Trusted Advisor (C) doesn't do ML bias. Comprehend (D) is NLP, not bias.
questions.pattern: 'ML bias analysis' + 'explainability' → SageMaker Clarify. Three capabilities: pre-training bias, post-training bias, feature attributions (SHAP). Model Registry integration for audit. This is an MLA-C01 flagship topic.

questions.scenario: A team's inference endpoint serves 50 requests/second average but with bursts up to 500/second during business hours. The model (400MB) runs on a single ml.g5.xlarge instance. Latency at burst reaches 1.5s (p99), causing timeouts. Cost-sensitive team — don't want to overprovision permanently. 24/7 service.
questions.question: Which scaling configuration MOST effectively addresses burst latency?
choices: SageMaker endpoint auto-scaling on InvocationsPerInstance target tracking, with appropriate min/max instances and cooldown configured to scale out before latency spikes.
Switch to SageMaker Serverless Inference for automatic scaling.
Keep single instance but increase instance size to ml.g5.16xlarge.
Add CloudFront in front of the endpoint for caching.
questions.explanation:
SageMaker real-time endpoints support auto-scaling with target tracking on InvocationsPerInstance (the SageMaker-specific metric). Configure min=1, max=N, and a reasonable target. Burst handling requires sensible cooldowns and scaling metrics. This scales horizontally for bursts without permanent overprovisioning.

Serverless Inference (B) has cold start latency (seconds) — worse for burst. Larger instance (C) is permanent overprovisioning. CloudFront (D) doesn't help inference uniqueness.
questions.pattern: SageMaker endpoint scaling: real-time + auto-scaling for traffic with bursts. Serverless is good for very intermittent (minutes between calls) traffic despite cold starts. Always-on high throughput → real-time with AS. Metric to use is InvocationsPerInstance (SageMaker's provided metric), not CPU.

questions.scenario: A team needs to train a large language model from scratch with 10B parameters. Training requires ~400 GPU-hours on H100-class hardware. The team has used SageMaker for small-model training but this is their first large distributed training job. They want model parallelism across multiple GPUs and nodes.
questions.question: Which SageMaker configuration BEST supports this training?
choices: SageMaker training on a single ml.p5 instance, running longer as needed.

SageMaker training with multi-instance distributed training using SageMaker Distributed Model Parallel or the SageMaker Training Toolkit with PyTorch FSDP / DeepSpeed, using ml.p5 instances in a cluster with EFA networking.
Amazon EMR with PySpark for LLM training.
SageMaker Canvas for no-code training.
questions.explanation:
Large model training requires distributed training with model parallelism (not just data parallelism) because a 10B-param model doesn't fit on one GPU. SageMaker supports this via its own SMDP library or via hosting PyTorch FSDP / DeepSpeed code. ml.p5 instances with EFA (Elastic Fabric Adapter) provide the high-bandwidth low-latency interconnect needed for large-scale distributed training.

Single instance (A) can't fit the model. EMR (C) is for big data, not LLM training. Canvas (D) is for AutoML on tabular data.
questions.pattern: Large model training: SageMaker distributed training + model parallel (SMDP, FSDP, DeepSpeed) + high-bandwidth networking (EFA). The exam tests whether you know EFA is the right interconnect for multi-node training and that model parallel differs from data parallel.

questions.scenario: A team labels 100,000 images for object detection. Budget is tight. Some labelers are in-house (accurate but slow), some are crowdsourced (faster but noisier). Quality matters — label errors hurt the model. They want an auditable labeling pipeline with quality checks.
questions.question: Which approach BEST balances cost, quality, and auditability?
choices: Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth with multi-labeler workflows (consensus-based labeling with worker agreement checks), pre-built object detection labeling templates, automated label verification with adjudication workflows for low-confidence labels, and CloudWatch metrics for labeler performance.
Amazon Mechanical Turk with custom labeling UI built from scratch.
Export images to a CSV for spreadsheet-based labeling by the in-house team only.
Use a Lambda function to auto-label images with Amazon Rekognition.
questions.explanation:
Ground Truth provides managed labeling with built-in templates (including object detection), worker consensus workflows (multiple labelers on same image, agreement checks), and adjudication workflows for disagreements. You can combine in-house (private workforce) and crowdsourced (Mechanical Turk) workforces in the same pipeline. Audit trail is native.

Raw MTurk (B) requires building everything. Spreadsheet (C) doesn't scale and lacks audit. Rekognition auto-labeling (D) is only OK if Rekognition's labels are what you want — often they're not.
questions.pattern: Quality-focused labeling at scale → Ground Truth with consensus workflows. Private workforce for sensitive, MTurk for scale, Vendor workforce for specialized. The exam tests whether you know Ground Truth supports multi-worker consensus out of the box — you don't have to build this.

questions.scenario: An inference workload processes 1M product images daily in overnight batches. Each image is scored by 3 different models (detection, classification, quality check). Images are 2-5MB. Results are written to DynamoDB. Current cost of real-time endpoints kept running 24/7 is excessive for a nightly-only workload.
questions.question: Which inference pattern MOST cost-effectively handles this?
choices: SageMaker Batch Transform jobs triggered on schedule, running the three models as sequential batch jobs on ephemeral compute that terminates after completion. Or a SageMaker Pipeline orchestrating the three batch transforms.
SageMaker real-time endpoints at minimum size, accepting long processing window.
Lambda functions invoking SageMaker real-time endpoints for each image.
SageMaker Serverless Inference invoking per image.
questions.explanation:
Batch Transform is purpose-built for periodic bulk inference: scheduled, ephemeral compute that spins up, processes the batch, and terminates. No cost between runs. Pipelines can orchestrate the three transforms with parallelism where possible.

Real-time minimum (B) still incurs 24/7 cost. Lambda + real-time (C) requires always-on endpoints. Serverless Inference (D) is for intermittent traffic with cold-start tolerance, not bulk batch — would be slower and potentially more expensive for 1M images.
questions.pattern: SageMaker inference choice: Real-time (sustained, low-latency), Serverless (intermittent, cold-start OK), Async (large payloads, queuing), Batch Transform (scheduled bulk, no latency requirement). Nightly bulk = Batch Transform. The exam tests whether you pick Batch over always-on for bulk workloads.

questions.scenario: A team needs to find the best hyperparameters for an XGBoost model. The search space includes 7 hyperparameters: learning rate (continuous log), max_depth (integer), num_round (integer), subsample (continuous), colsample_bytree (continuous), gamma (continuous log), and min_child_weight (continuous log). Budget allows ~100 training runs.
questions.question: Which tuning strategy MOST efficiently finds good hyperparameters?
choices: SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning with Bayesian strategy. Defines the 7 hyperparameter ranges with appropriate scaling (log for learning rate and gamma), objective metric to optimize, and max jobs budget. Bayesian tuning learns from previous trials to pick next trials efficiently.
Grid search with 3 values per hyperparameter (2187 combinations).
Random search with 100 random samples from each range.
Run 100 trials with default hyperparameters to establish a baseline, then manually tune.
questions.explanation:
SageMaker AMT (Automatic Model Tuning) with Bayesian strategy uses results of previous trials to pick subsequent ones more intelligently than random or grid. With 7 hyperparameters and 100 trials, Bayesian converges much faster than random. Proper scaling (log scale for parameters that range orders of magnitude like learning rate) matters for efficient search.

Grid search (B) exceeds budget (2187 > 100). Random search (C) works but is less efficient than Bayesian. Default baseline (D) doesn't search.
questions.pattern: Hyperparameter tuning choice: Grid (small search spaces), Random (medium), Bayesian (large/expensive search spaces). SageMaker AMT supports all three. With 7+ HPs and limited budget, Bayesian is the expected answer. Don't forget to mark log-scale parameters as log-scaled.

questions.scenario: An ML engineer is debugging a model whose predictions look fine on average but are catastrophically wrong for a small subset of inputs. They suspect certain input ranges or feature combinations produce bad predictions. They want to analyze inference requests and responses captured in production, correlate with business outcomes, and slice by features to find the failure patterns.
questions.question: Which capability MOST efficiently supports this debugging?
choices: SageMaker Data Capture on the endpoint to log requests and responses to S3. Then analyze the captured data with SageMaker Clarify or notebook-based slicing to find feature ranges where model performance drops, potentially feeding back into Model Monitor baselines.
CloudWatch Logs for the endpoint; grep for errors.
Increase endpoint timeout and hope the issue goes away.
Retrain on all available data without investigating.
questions.explanation:
Data Capture logs every inference request and response to S3, which becomes a dataset you can analyze. Slice by feature ranges, join with business outcomes, run Clarify or ad-hoc analysis to find where the model fails. This is the MLA-C01 canonical production debugging pattern.

CloudWatch Logs (B) show errors but not inference data. Timeout increase (C) doesn't help wrong predictions. Blind retraining (D) doesn't diagnose.
questions.pattern: 'Debug production ML' or 'analyze inference in production' → Data Capture. It's the raw material for Model Monitor but also a standalone debugging tool. Without Data Capture enabled, you're flying blind.

questions.scenario: A team builds a recommendation model. Source data sits in 5 systems: user profiles in DynamoDB, transactions in Aurora, product catalog in DocumentDB, behavioral events in S3 (JSON lines), and third-party enrichment data in S3 (Parquet). They need to join these for training sets without operational impact on source systems.
questions.question: Which approach BEST enables training data assembly across sources?
choices: Direct queries from the training code to each source system.

Nightly exports to a data lake in S3 (via DMS for Aurora, AWS Glue connectors for DynamoDB and DocumentDB, existing S3 data stays in place). Use AWS Glue crawlers to catalog, then Athena or Glue ETL to join into training datasets. Lake Formation for access governance.
Replicate all sources to Redshift for SQL-based joins.
Copy all data to a local SSD on the training instance.
questions.explanation:
Data lake pattern minimizes impact on operational sources: DMS for Aurora (CDC or full-load), Glue DynamoDB and DocumentDB connectors, existing S3 data stays in place. Glue Crawler builds the catalog. Athena or Glue joins across sources. Lake Formation governs access. This is the canonical analytics-for-ML pattern and decouples ML from operational systems.

Direct source queries (A) impacts production. Redshift (C) works but is more infra than needed and doesn't natively query S3. Local SSD (D) doesn't scale and isn't reproducible.
questions.pattern: 'Assemble ML training data from multiple sources' → data lake with Glue/DMS ingestion + Glue Catalog + Athena/Glue for joins + Lake Formation for governance. The exam favors data lake patterns over direct source access or Redshift consolidation.

references.title: Building a data lake on AWS

questions.scenario: A team trains time-series forecasting models for demand forecasting across 500 products. They try an LSTM and a classical ARIMA baseline. They want to consider an AWS purpose-built forecasting option that might outperform both without significant custom development.
questions.question: Which AWS service BEST fits this need?
choices: Amazon Forecast (managed time-series forecasting with algorithms like DeepAR+, CNN-QR, Prophet, ARIMA, ETS — the service compares and selects the best). Note: check current availability as AWS service portfolios evolve.
SageMaker with only built-in XGBoost algorithm.
Amazon Comprehend for time-series forecasting.
Amazon QuickSight for forecasting.
questions.explanation:
Amazon Forecast is a managed service for time-series forecasting that benchmarks multiple algorithms (DeepAR+, CNN-QR, Prophet, ARIMA, ETS, NPTS) and selects the best. It handles cold-start and related-time-series data. Important caveat: AWS announced that Amazon Forecast would no longer accept new customers as of a certain date — check current availability; the SageMaker JumpStart or SageMaker Canvas forecasting capabilities may now be the preferred path.

XGBoost (B) can do forecasting but requires significant feature engineering. Comprehend (C) is NLP. QuickSight (D) does basic forecasting but not model selection across algorithms.
questions.pattern: 'Managed time-series forecasting' traditionally → Amazon Forecast. Be aware of service lifecycle changes — AWS has been consolidating some AI services. If Forecast appears, it's still a valid exam answer for the forecasting use case. Always check the AWS service availability at exam time.
questions.note: As of late 2024, AWS announced restrictions on new Amazon Forecast sign-ups and pushed users toward SageMaker Canvas for time-series. For exam purposes, Amazon Forecast is still a valid answer choice, but worth knowing the landscape is shifting.

references.title: Time series with AWS AI services

questions.scenario: A team's SageMaker training jobs repeatedly fail at different points. Sometimes it's out-of-memory, sometimes NaN loss, sometimes the job just hangs. They want to diagnose these issues without manually inspecting thousands of lines of training logs.
questions.question: Which capability BEST supports training job debugging?
choices: Amazon SageMaker Debugger — captures tensor values during training, runs built-in rules that flag problems like vanishing/exploding gradients, NaN/infinity, overfitting, loss not decreasing, etc. Integrates with training jobs and emits notifications.
Increase CloudWatch log retention to 90 days.
Run training on a larger instance; most issues are resource issues.
Rewrite training code in Go for better debugging.
questions.explanation:
SageMaker Debugger is purpose-built for training diagnostics: captures tensor data during training (model parameters, gradients, activations), runs built-in rules (vanishing/exploding gradients, NaN/infinity, class imbalance at training time, overfitting, not decreasing loss), and can stop jobs or notify on rule violations. No manual log inspection.

CloudWatch retention (B) doesn't help diagnose. Larger instance (C) only fixes OOM. Go (D) isn't relevant.
questions.pattern: 'Training job debugging' → SageMaker Debugger. Built-in rules cover common training pathologies. Often appears with 'NaN loss,' 'gradient issues,' 'training not converging,' or 'OOM.' It's the MLA-C01 expected answer for training-time diagnostics.

questions.scenario: A model is deployed to an endpoint that handles public user-facing predictions. Security review mandates: model inputs must be validated against injection attacks, inference requests must have authentication tied to the calling user, rate limits per user identity, and request/response encryption.
questions.question: Which architecture BEST addresses these front-end concerns for an ML endpoint?
choices: Expose the SageMaker endpoint directly to the public internet with IAM authentication.

API Gateway in front of the SageMaker endpoint (invoked via Lambda or direct integration). API Gateway handles authentication (Cognito or IAM), per-client rate limiting via usage plans, input validation via request validators or Lambda authorizer, and TLS. WAF on API Gateway for additional request inspection.
ALB in front of the endpoint with security groups restricting source IPs.
CloudFront for TLS termination and caching of predictions.
questions.explanation:
SageMaker endpoints aren't front-end-friendly by themselves. API Gateway provides auth (Cognito user pools or IAM), rate limiting via usage plans, input validation (request validators and Lambda authorizers), TLS, and WAF integration for injection protection. This is the canonical ML-API architecture for public endpoints.

Direct exposure (A) skips all the front-end protections. ALB (C) lacks usage plans and native validation. CloudFront caching (D) is wrong for inference — each request is typically unique.
questions.pattern: Public-facing ML endpoint architecture: API Gateway + (Lambda or direct) + SageMaker endpoint. API Gateway provides the request-level security plumbing that SageMaker endpoints don't. WAF for additional request inspection. The exam expects you to layer these services.

references.title: API Gateway direct integration with SageMaker
API Gateway usage plans and API keys

questions.scenario: A team has 800 GB of raw data in S3 for training an ML model. Training jobs currently copy all data to the training instance's EBS volume at job start, which takes 40 minutes. They want to reduce this data-loading overhead for frequent training experiments.
questions.question: Which approach MOST effectively reduces data-loading overhead?
choices: Copy the data to a larger EBS volume attached to each new training job.

Use SageMaker training input modes. File mode (current) downloads before training starts. Pipe mode streams data directly from S3 to training containers during training. FastFile mode provides S3 random access without upfront download. FSx for Lustre as the training input for shared low-latency access across jobs.
Reduce the training dataset size to fit in RAM.
Move the training data to Glacier to reduce S3 cost.
questions.explanation:
SageMaker training has four input modes: File (default, downloads all), Pipe (streams at training time), FastFile (on-demand S3 access, good random access), and FSx for Lustre (shared POSIX filesystem, ideal for repeated training runs). For 800 GB with frequent experiments, FastFile or FSx for Lustre eliminates the 40-minute setup. FSx for Lustre is especially good when the same data is used across many jobs.

Larger EBS (A) doesn't eliminate the download. Reducing data (C) hurts model quality. Glacier (D) makes training infeasible.
questions.pattern: Training data input modes: File (simple, small datasets), Pipe (sequential access, very large datasets streamed), FastFile (random access without full download), FSx for Lustre (shared across jobs, high throughput). When 'training startup slow' or 'large dataset' appears, expect input mode choices in the answers.

references.title: Using FSx for Lustre with SageMaker

questions.scenario: A team builds a text classification model on customer support tickets. They want good performance without training a transformer from scratch. They have 20,000 labeled tickets in English and Spanish.
questions.question: Which approach MOST efficiently builds a strong classifier?
choices: Train a custom transformer from scratch on the 20,000 tickets.

Use a pre-trained language model (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa, or a multilingual variant for English/Spanish support) via SageMaker JumpStart; fine-tune on the 20,000 tickets. This transfer-learning approach gives strong results with limited data.
Use Amazon Comprehend custom classification — a managed service for training text classifiers without ML infrastructure.
Both B and C are viable; choose JumpStart for more control over architecture and fine-tuning, Comprehend Custom for faster time-to-result without managing SageMaker.
questions.explanation:
Multiple valid answers — and the exam sometimes has 'choose between two good options' questions. JumpStart fine-tuning gives maximum control and accuracy potential. Comprehend Custom Classification is managed, requires minimal ML expertise, and is often sufficient. Both beat from-scratch transformer training (A) for 20k labels. The 'both viable' answer is correct when it genuinely is.

From-scratch transformer (A) needs millions of examples. JumpStart alone (B) or Comprehend Custom alone (C) are each valid but not the BEST answer because the real answer depends on team expertise and control requirements.
questions.pattern: Text classification choices: Comprehend Custom (managed, fast, limited control) vs SageMaker JumpStart fine-tuning (more setup, more control). From-scratch transformer training is almost always wrong at <100k examples. Transfer learning rules for NLP.

references.title: Fine-tuning foundation models with SageMaker JumpStart

questions.scenario: An ML inference workload uses a large deep learning model (8GB). The model runs on ml.g5.4xlarge instances. Inference throughput is 200/sec per instance. Latency requirement: p99 under 100ms. The team wants to reduce cost without hurting latency or throughput.
questions.question: Which optimization MOST likely reduces cost while maintaining performance?
choices: Model compilation with SageMaker Neo (compiles model for target hardware), quantization from fp32 to int8 or fp16, and potentially switch to AWS Inferentia (Inf2) instances which are purpose-built for inference at lower cost than GPU instances.
Cache predictions in ElastiCache.
Move to CPU instances exclusively.
Use larger GPU instances for better throughput per dollar.
questions.explanation:
Inference optimization toolkit: Neo compilation (hardware-specific optimizations), quantization (int8/fp16 reduces compute and memory), and custom silicon (Inferentia) for inference at ~70% lower cost than comparable GPUs. Pick the combo matching your accuracy/latency budget.

ElastiCache (B) only works if inputs repeat — rare for production inference. CPU-only (C) is likely too slow for 100ms on 8GB DL model. Larger GPU (D) is more expensive, not cheaper.
questions.pattern: Inference cost optimization: compilation (Neo), quantization, purpose-built hardware (Inferentia, Trainium). Inferentia is AWS's silicon for inference, much cheaper than GPU when your model supports it. Always consider the full stack.

questions.scenario: A team implements a regression model predicting house prices. Mean Absolute Error is their current metric. Business wants to penalize large errors more (a $100K error on one house is worse than 10 x $10K errors). They also want to compare model performance across neighborhoods of different price ranges.
questions.question: Which metric choice BEST addresses these business requirements?
choices: Continue with MAE since it's interpretable.

Use RMSE (penalizes large errors more than MAE due to squaring) as the primary metric. Use MAPE or Mean Absolute Percentage Error for cross-neighborhood comparison since it normalizes by the magnitude of the target.
Use R-squared since it's between 0 and 1.
Use classification metrics like F1 score.
questions.explanation:
Metric selection matches business need. RMSE's squaring penalizes large errors more than MAE — matches the business requirement. MAPE expresses error as a percentage, enabling fair comparison across neighborhoods with vastly different price ranges (a $50K error is different in $200K vs $2M neighborhoods).

MAE alone (A) doesn't penalize large errors enough. R-squared (C) is a fit measure, not an error metric. F1 (D) is classification.
questions.pattern: Regression metrics: MAE (equal weight all errors), MSE/RMSE (penalize large errors), MAPE (scale-invariant via percentages), R² (variance explained). Pick based on whether outliers matter (MAE vs RMSE) and whether scale normalization is needed (MAPE).

questions.scenario: A team builds a recommendation model as a monthly batch job. After 4 months of operation, they notice the model performs well on the first day after retraining but degrades by month-end. They suspect concept drift (user preferences shifting within a month). They want to automatically retrain when drift is detected rather than on a fixed schedule.
questions.question: Which architecture BEST automates drift-triggered retraining?
choices: SageMaker Model Monitor detecting drift, publishing to EventBridge on rule violations. EventBridge triggers a SageMaker Pipeline execution for retraining. Pipeline registers the new model in Model Registry with an approval step before deployment. Model Registry approval triggers deployment via EventBridge + Lambda/CodePipeline.
Retrain the model daily to avoid drift.
A Lambda function that runs daily and checks metrics manually.
Retrain based on calendar (1st of each month) as currently.
questions.explanation:
Drift-triggered retraining is the event-driven MLOps pattern: Model Monitor detects drift → EventBridge → SageMaker Pipelines retrains → Model Registry captures new version with approval step → deployment triggered by approval. This matches the business need for retraining when drift occurs rather than on a calendar.

Daily retraining (B) is wasteful. Manual Lambda (C) rebuilds Model Monitor. Calendar-based (D) is what they want to get away from.
questions.pattern: Automated MLOps cycle: Model Monitor (detect) → EventBridge (event) → Pipelines (retrain) → Model Registry (version + approve) → deployment automation. The exam tests whether you can assemble this full loop.

questions.scenario: A team ingests 10TB of CSV data daily from vendors. They want to query it with Athena, but CSV queries are slow and expensive. They also want to accelerate downstream ML feature engineering that scans this data.
questions.question: Which optimization MOST efficiently improves Athena and downstream ML performance?
choices: Convert CSV to Parquet with Snappy compression (or ORC) using AWS Glue ETL or Athena CTAS. Partition the data by date in S3. Use columnar reads in downstream ML feature engineering via pyarrow or Spark.
Load everything into Redshift for faster querying.
Use S3 Transfer Acceleration to speed up uploads.
Compress the CSVs with gzip to reduce size.
questions.explanation:
Parquet with partitioning is the canonical analytics-data-format answer. Columnar format means Athena scans only needed columns (huge cost savings — Athena is priced by data scanned). Partitioning means only matching partitions are scanned. Snappy or zstd compression reduces size without CPU overhead.

Redshift (B) adds infra; Athena+Parquet is more ML-friendly. Transfer Acceleration (C) is for upload speed, not query speed. Gzipped CSV (D) is still row-oriented — scan cost remains high.
questions.pattern: Analytics data format: CSV/JSON → Parquet/ORC with compression (Snappy/zstd) + partitioning. Reduces Athena cost and ML feature engineering time. Glue can do the conversion as an ETL job.

references.title: Converting to columnar formats with Athena CTAS

questions.scenario: A team needs to build a classifier but only has a small labeled dataset (500 examples). They have a much larger unlabeled dataset (50,000 examples) and can request limited additional labels from human annotators (~1,000 more labels).
questions.question: Which ML approach MOST effectively leverages the unlabeled data and limited labeling budget?
choices: Train only on the 500 labeled examples.

Semi-supervised learning using the unlabeled data, combined with active learning (via SageMaker Ground Truth Plus or similar) to select the 1,000 most-informative examples for human labeling. Transfer learning from pre-trained models where applicable.
Use SageMaker AutoPilot on the 500 examples.
Use generative AI to create synthetic labels for all 50,000 unlabeled examples.
questions.explanation:
Active learning: the model identifies its least-certain predictions and requests labels for those specifically, maximizing information gain per label. Semi-supervised learning uses unlabeled data directly (consistency regularization, pseudo-labeling). Transfer learning gives a strong starting point. Combination is the strongest approach for small-label regimes.

Only 500 labels (A) ignores available resources. AutoPilot on 500 (C) will overfit. Synthetic labels from LLM (D) can introduce systematic bias.
questions.pattern: Small-labeled-data patterns: (1) transfer learning (pre-trained models), (2) semi-supervised (use unlabeled), (3) active learning (smart labeling), (4) data augmentation. Combine as applicable. Naive ML on 500 examples is almost never the right answer.

questions.scenario: An ML team wants to version control every element of their ML pipeline: training data snapshots, model artifacts, training code, training hyperparameters, and which dataset version produced which model. An auditor may request 'show me exactly how model version 2.3 was produced.'
questions.question: Which combination BEST provides full ML lineage?
choices: Git for code; trust that training jobs are reproducible.

SageMaker Pipelines for orchestration (executions logged), Model Registry for model versioning with metadata linking to training jobs, SageMaker ML Lineage Tracking (automatic for Pipelines) linking data, code, and models. Git for code versioning. S3 versioning for data snapshots. CodeCommit or GitHub for training code.
Store everything in a custom DynamoDB table.
Rely on CloudTrail for all audit needs.
questions.explanation:
ML lineage covers code (Git), data (S3 versioning or dataset manifests), parameters (Pipelines execution), artifacts (Model Registry), and relationships (ML Lineage Tracking). SageMaker Pipelines and Model Registry automatically create lineage entities and associations — you don't have to build tracking yourself.

Git alone (A) misses data and artifact lineage. Custom DynamoDB (C) rebuilds what Lineage Tracking provides. CloudTrail (D) records API calls, not ML lineage.
questions.pattern: ML lineage stack: Pipelines + Model Registry + ML Lineage Tracking + Git (code) + S3 versioning (data). The exam tests whether you can identify the lineage-specific features like ML Lineage Tracking (separate from CloudTrail).

questions.scenario: A data team receives 2 TB of raw sensor data daily. Before ML, the data needs quality checks: completeness (no missing readings), format validation (correct types and ranges), and duplicate detection. Failing records must be flagged and either fixed or routed to a dead-letter location. The team wants minimal custom code.
questions.question: Which service BEST supports this data quality pipeline?
choices: AWS Glue Data Quality (or Glue DataBrew data quality rules) defining rules for completeness, range, uniqueness, etc. Failing records route to a DLQ bucket. Passing records proceed to the ML feature pipeline. Integrates with Glue ETL and Data Catalog.
Manual pandas scripts run weekly.
Amazon Athena with custom SQL checks.
Amazon Macie for data quality.
questions.explanation:
Glue Data Quality provides managed rule-based data quality with Deequ under the hood: built-in rules for completeness, uniqueness, ranges, patterns, referential integrity, etc. Integrates into ETL jobs. DataBrew also has data quality profiling. These reduce custom code vs building quality checks from scratch.

Manual pandas (B) is custom code. Athena SQL (C) works but lacks managed rule framework. Macie (D) is for PII detection, not data quality.
questions.pattern: Data quality at scale → Glue Data Quality or DataBrew. Rule-based, managed, integrated with Glue ETL. Exam often includes Macie as a distractor — Macie is specifically for sensitive data discovery, not general quality.

questions.scenario: A team trains many similar models — one per product category, 50 categories total. Currently each is a separate SageMaker training job. This is operationally painful. They want a way to orchestrate all 50 jobs with shared common infrastructure, parallel execution where possible, and results aggregation.
questions.question: Which approach BEST scales this multi-model workflow?
choices: Write a bash script that kicks off 50 training jobs in sequence.

SageMaker Pipelines with a loop or parallel branches for the 50 models, or a single SageMaker training job that trains all 50 models in one process if they share code — e.g., using Step Functions Map state. SageMaker Experiments for tracking all runs as trials in one experiment.
SageMaker AutoPilot on one combined dataset.
Create 50 separate SageMaker notebooks, one per category.
questions.explanation:
Parallel multi-model training needs orchestration: Step Functions Map state enables dynamic parallel execution; SageMaker Pipelines with parallel branches is alternative. SageMaker Experiments groups all 50 runs as trials within one experiment for comparison. AutoPilot per category is another valid option but single combined AutoPilot (C) treats categories as features rather than training per-category models.

Bash script (A) is sequential. Combined AutoPilot (C) solves a different problem. 50 notebooks (D) are unscalable.
questions.pattern: Parallel ML job orchestration → Step Functions Map or Pipelines with parallel steps. Experiments for tracking many runs. SageMaker Training Compiler can also help. The exam tests whether you know SageMaker has per-run comparison and grouping via Experiments.

questions.scenario: A team is building a document understanding pipeline: extract text and tables from scanned invoices, classify invoice types, and extract entities like vendor, date, amount. They don't want to train custom models unless necessary.
questions.question: Which combination BEST handles this end-to-end?
choices: Train custom models in SageMaker for every step.

Amazon Textract for text and table extraction from scanned documents. Amazon Comprehend for entity recognition and custom classification. AWS Lambda or Step Functions to orchestrate. Only fall back to custom SageMaker models if Textract/Comprehend don't meet accuracy needs on specific fields.
Amazon Rekognition for invoice understanding.
Amazon Transcribe for invoice text.
questions.explanation:
Managed AI services are the right default for common ML tasks: Textract for OCR and structured extraction from documents (tables, forms, queries), Comprehend for entities and custom classification. Step Functions orchestrates. Fall back to custom only for specialized accuracy needs. This avoids unnecessary model training and maintenance.

Custom-everything (A) is overkill. Rekognition (C) is image classification/detection, not document text. Transcribe (D) is speech-to-text.
questions.pattern: Managed AI service map: Textract (OCR/document), Comprehend (NLP entities/classification), Rekognition (image/video), Transcribe (speech-to-text), Translate (translation), Polly (text-to-speech), Kendra (enterprise search). Pick the right managed service first; only build custom for domain-specific or accuracy-critical pieces.

questions.scenario: A production ML model is making predictions used for automated high-value business decisions (loan approvals). Rare catastrophic prediction errors have caused regulatory scrutiny. The team needs automatic detection of inputs likely to cause errors (out-of-distribution inputs) and a human-review fallback for suspicious cases.
questions.question: Which architecture BEST addresses this risk?
choices: Accept errors as inherent to ML and handle them post-hoc.

Add a confidence-threshold gate at inference time: predictions with confidence below a threshold or flagged as out-of-distribution (via the model's confidence score or a separate OOD detector) route to Amazon Augmented AI (A2I) for human review. Model Monitor catches drift that might increase OOD rate. Log all overrides for audit.
Retrain the model on more data.
Disable the automated decisions entirely.
questions.explanation:
Human-in-the-loop with A2I is the AWS-canonical answer for high-stakes predictions. Confidence-threshold gating triggers human review for low-confidence or OOD cases. A2I provides workflows, worker management, and audit trail. Model Monitor catches distribution shifts that might increase low-confidence cases.

Accept errors (A) fails regulatory scrutiny. More training data (C) doesn't solve OOD — rare cases remain rare. Disabling (D) abandons the business value.
questions.pattern: High-stakes ML + low-confidence handling → Amazon A2I. Trigger on confidence threshold or explicit OOD detection. A2I integrates with SageMaker, Rekognition, Textract and supports custom workflows. Regulatory/compliance scenarios especially favor A2I.

questions.scenario: A team's training data has class imbalance, outliers, and some columns with high cardinality categoricals (tens of thousands of unique values). They want robust preprocessing that handles all these before training a gradient boosted model.
questions.question: Which preprocessing recipe BEST handles these data issues?
choices: Drop minority classes, outliers, and high-cardinality columns to simplify.

Handle class imbalance with SMOTE or class weights (not dropping). Handle outliers with robust scaling or capping (not dropping). Handle high-cardinality categoricals with target encoding, hashing, or embedding (not one-hot). Apply via Data Wrangler transformations or a SageMaker Processing job for reproducibility.
One-hot encode all categoricals including the high-cardinality ones.
Use only numerical features and drop all categoricals.
questions.explanation:
Data issues need data-aware handling: class imbalance → resample or class weights (don't drop minority). Outliers → robust scaling or capping based on domain (don't auto-drop). High-cardinality categoricals → target encoding, hashing, or embedding (one-hot creates thousands of sparse features). Data Wrangler or Processing jobs for reproducibility.

Drop everything (A) destroys signal. Full one-hot (C) explodes feature space. Drop all categoricals (D) throws away signal.
questions.pattern: Data issue handling: class imbalance (SMOTE, class weights), outliers (robust scaling, capping, not always dropping), high-cardinality (target encoding, hashing, embeddings). The exam tests whether you know the right technique per issue — blanket drop-or-one-hot is usually wrong.

questions.scenario: A team uses SageMaker Autopilot for tabular classification. They want to understand not just the best model but also which features mattered most, how the model was built, and to customize parts of the generated pipeline for domain-specific transformations.
questions.question: Which Autopilot feature BEST supports these needs?
choices: Autopilot produces only opaque models with no interpretability.

Autopilot in 'ensemble' mode for best accuracy, or 'HPO' mode for more introspectable training. Either mode produces candidate notebooks with the full generated code — use these as starting points for customization. Explainability via Clarify integration is automatic. Feature importance comes with the model.
Autopilot cannot be customized; use only raw SageMaker training.
Autopilot outputs are only JSON with predictions.
questions.explanation:
Autopilot is not a black box. It produces 'candidate notebooks' showing the full generated pipeline (feature engineering, algorithm selection, hyperparameters) which you can inspect and customize. It includes Clarify-based explainability automatically. HPO mode gives one model at a time (more inspectable); Ensemble mode aggregates for accuracy. You can customize the pipeline by taking the candidate notebook as a starting point.

Opaque (A) is wrong. No customization (C) is wrong. Predictions only (D) is wrong.
questions.pattern: Autopilot capabilities: candidate notebooks (full pipeline code), Clarify explainability, HPO vs Ensemble modes, customization starting point. Often the exam distractor is 'Autopilot is a black box' which is false.

questions.scenario: A team's model endpoint has variable latency. During high load, p99 latency spikes from 40ms to 800ms while p50 stays at 30ms. CPU utilization is below 40% during these spikes. The model is small (200MB). They want to understand and fix the latency tail.
questions.question: Which diagnostic and solution BEST addresses this?
choices: Buy a larger instance; latency spikes mean CPU is overloaded.

Enable SageMaker Inference Recommender and endpoint-level profiling. Likely culprits given symptoms: concurrency queueing at the inference container (increase model server threads, adjust batch size), GC pauses (review JVM/Python settings), or disk I/O for cold payloads. Consider SageMaker multi-model or multi-container endpoints if multiple models share the endpoint.
Cache all predictions to avoid cold latency.
Reduce logging to improve performance.
questions.explanation:
Low CPU + high tail latency is a classic symptom of concurrency/queueing or GC pauses, not compute capacity. SageMaker Inference Recommender helps benchmark configurations. Common fixes: tune model server worker threads (e.g., gunicorn workers, TorchServe batch/worker settings), adjust GC settings, check I/O.

Larger instance (A) misdiagnoses — CPU is already underutilized. Caching (C) is a patch, not diagnosis. Reduce logging (D) is a guess.
questions.pattern: Latency debugging: p99 spikes with low CPU → concurrency/queueing or GC, not capacity. SageMaker Inference Recommender for benchmarking. The exam expects diagnostic reasoning, not blanket upgrades.

questions.scenario: A team wants to use generative AI in their application. Requirements: use Anthropic Claude as the foundation model, customize it for their domain with their proprietary documents, keep proprietary docs out of the model vendor's training data, and meet enterprise data protection standards.
questions.question: Which architecture BEST meets these requirements?
choices: Upload proprietary docs to the public Claude web interface.

Amazon Bedrock to access Claude (data not used to train vendor models; in-AWS). Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with Bedrock Knowledge Bases (or custom vector store like OpenSearch Serverless, Aurora pgvector) to incorporate proprietary documents without fine-tuning. IAM and VPC controls for data protection. Guardrails for Bedrock to filter inputs/outputs.
Fine-tune Claude on proprietary docs via an API.
Build a custom transformer from scratch for this use case.
questions.explanation:
Bedrock is AWS's managed foundation model service — data sent to FMs via Bedrock isn't used to train vendor models. RAG (via Bedrock Knowledge Bases or a custom vector store) lets you use proprietary data at inference without fine-tuning. Bedrock Guardrails filter unsafe content. IAM and VPC endpoints for data protection.

Public Claude web (A) violates data protection. Fine-tuning Claude (C) isn't available via public API the way you'd want — Bedrock customization is limited and evolving. From-scratch (D) is absurd.
questions.pattern: Enterprise generative AI → Amazon Bedrock + RAG (Knowledge Bases or custom vector store) + Guardrails. Bedrock is the enterprise-safe FM gateway. RAG is usually preferred over fine-tuning for document-grounded use cases.

questions.scenario: An ML team wants centralized governance: all models must be reviewed before deployment, access to production endpoints is restricted by role, deployment is repeatable via CI/CD, and the Model Registry is the single source of truth for approved models. Teams currently deploy ad-hoc from notebooks.
questions.question: Which governance pattern BEST meets these requirements?
choices: Manual Model Registry entries plus Slack approvals.

SageMaker Model Registry with ApprovalStatus workflow (Pending → Approved/Rejected). Deployment infrastructure (CI/CD via CodePipeline or SageMaker Projects) triggers on ApprovalStatus change. IAM roles restrict deployment actions to the deployment pipeline identity. Human review happens via console or API approval of Model Registry entries.
Block all deployments; require VP approval for every change.
Deploy everything via Lambda; no approval needed.
questions.explanation:
Model Registry with ApprovalStatus is the AWS-native model governance pattern. Status transitions (Pending → Approved/Rejected) drive CI/CD. SageMaker Projects provide templates for this pattern. IAM restricts who can approve and who can deploy (different roles). This combines human gates with automation.

Manual + Slack (A) lacks audit and automation. VP approval for every change (C) is operationally dead. Free-for-all Lambda (D) violates governance.
questions.pattern: Model governance: Model Registry + ApprovalStatus + CI/CD (CodePipeline, SageMaker Projects) + IAM segregation of duties. SageMaker Projects provide MLOps templates for this. The exam tests whether you understand the approval-driven automation pattern.

questions.scenario: A team wants to monitor their deployed model for multiple concerns at once: data drift on inputs, model quality drift (are predictions matching ground truth that arrives later), bias drift (are predictions becoming unfair over time), and feature attribution drift (has the relative importance of features changed). They want alerts on any of these.
questions.question: Which Model Monitor setup BEST covers all four concerns?
choices: Only Data Quality monitoring; it covers everything.

Enable all four Model Monitor types: Data Quality (input drift, no labels needed), Model Quality (requires ground truth labels), Bias Drift (requires Clarify baseline), Feature Attribution Drift (requires SHAP baseline). Each has baseline from training data. Schedule runs and set CloudWatch alarms on drift metrics.
Only Model Quality; labels arrive eventually.
One custom CloudWatch metric tracking average prediction.
questions.explanation:
Model Monitor has four distinct monitor types, each addressing a specific drift concern: Data Quality (input distributions), Model Quality (actual accuracy when labels arrive), Bias Drift (Clarify-based fairness metrics over time), Feature Attribution Drift (SHAP-based importance shifts). Each requires a baseline captured from training data. Run on a schedule with CloudWatch alarms.

Data Quality only (A) misses prediction-vs-truth. Model Quality only (C) misses input drift. Custom metric (D) doesn't capture distribution or bias.
questions.pattern: Model Monitor four types: Data Quality (no labels), Model Quality (labels required), Bias Drift (Clarify-linked), Feature Attribution Drift (SHAP-linked). Know which require labels. Know which require Clarify baselines. The exam tests whether you can pick the right monitor per concern.

questions.scenario: A team has a SageMaker real-time endpoint serving a 1.2GB image classification model. Traffic is 100 req/sec steady with occasional 10x bursts for 2-5 minutes. They evaluate three configurations: (A) single ml.g4dn.xlarge with auto-scaling 1-5 instances, (B) SageMaker Serverless Inference with max concurrency 50, (C) ml.c6i.4xlarge instance with multi-container endpoint hosting two model copies. First-invocation latency matters — model initialization takes 45 seconds from cold. p99 SLA is 300ms during steady state.
questions.question: Which configuration BEST fits the requirements?
choices: SageMaker Serverless Inference — auto-scales perfectly for bursty traffic with no idle cost.

Single ml.g4dn.xlarge with auto-scaling: provides GPU-accelerated inference for image classification (CPU is typically 10x slower for vision models), auto-scaling handles bursts, pre-warmed instances avoid cold-start latency during steady state. Accept that new scale-out instances take 45s to warm.
ml.c6i.4xlarge CPU instance with multi-container endpoint: cheaper per hour than GPU.
AWS Lambda with the model in a container image.
questions.explanation:
The trap: Serverless Inference (A) has cold-start penalties measured in seconds. A 45-second model init means first-request-after-idle latency will blow the 300ms SLA badly. Serverless is best for sporadic traffic where occasional cold starts are acceptable. 'Traffic is 100 req/sec steady' means you're never idle — so Serverless's main benefit (scale-to-zero) doesn't apply, and its cold-start penalty hurts.

Option C (CPU) is a trap for image classification — GPUs are typically 10x faster for vision models, so CPU-based inference likely fails p99 SLA anyway. Option D (Lambda) has the same cold-start issue plus a 15-minute execution limit isn't the concern here but memory/runtime constraints for a 1.2GB model are. Option B's GPU with auto-scaling is boring but correct — steady traffic keeps instances warm, and bursts trigger scale-out even with 45s warm time because the initial instances absorb the burst.
questions.pattern: SageMaker inference choice depends on traffic shape AND cold-start tolerance: Real-time + auto-scaling = sustained traffic with bursts, low latency. Serverless = sporadic traffic with cold-start tolerance (seconds). Async = large payloads, long-running, queue-based. Batch = scheduled bulk. If model init is slow and traffic is steady, Serverless is WRONG — people pick it because 'serverless = modern' but the cold-start math often doesn't work.

questions.scenario: A team trains a fraud detection model on tabular data with 1% fraud class. After SMOTE oversampling to 1:1 ratio, XGBoost training, and threshold tuning, the model achieves: 92% precision and 87% recall on holdout data. In production, recall drops to 64% despite precision staying at 91%. Data Wrangler shows the feature distributions in production match training. Ground truth labels confirm more fraud is occurring than being caught.
questions.question: What is the MOST LIKELY cause of the production recall drop, and the best investigation?
choices: Data drift — investigate feature distribution changes using SageMaker Model Monitor Data Quality.

SMOTE-induced train-test leakage: SMOTE was applied before splitting into train/validation, creating synthetic samples that appeared in both train and validation. The holdout metrics were optimistic because validation contained near-duplicates of training data. Re-run the pipeline applying SMOTE ONLY to the training split, and validate on untouched real data.
Hyperparameter drift — the production XGBoost model uses different hyperparameters than training.
The threshold tuned on validation doesn't apply to production traffic; retune on production data.
questions.explanation:
This is a classic SMOTE mistake. SMOTE creates synthetic minority samples based on existing minority samples. If you apply SMOTE before splitting train/validation, some synthetic samples in validation are near-duplicates of training samples — your validation metrics are artificially inflated. In production, where data is 'real,' the model performs closer to what it would have without the leakage.

Option A is possible but the scenario says distributions match training. Option C doesn't happen — hyperparameters are baked into the trained model. Option D doesn't explain why validation was 87% recall but production is 64% — the real fix is to re-run the pipeline correctly.
questions.pattern: SMOTE and other resampling techniques MUST be applied AFTER train/test split, only to the training split. Same rule applies to any data transformation that uses multiple rows (e.g., target encoding). Applying before split causes data leakage and optimistic validation metrics. This is a common ML pipeline bug the exam tests.

questions.scenario: A team is designing a feature pipeline for a recommendation model. Features include: user embedding (vector of 256 floats, updates daily), user recent activity (last 50 events, updates on every user action), product catalog features (updates weekly via batch), and real-time session signals (updates on every request). Inference latency budget: 50ms end-to-end including feature fetch. Feature Store is chosen. Which storage decisions BEST fit each feature group?
questions.question: Which feature storage configuration MOST effectively handles these access patterns?
choices: Single Feature Store feature group with online store enabled for all features; offline store for training backup.

Four feature groups with appropriate storage: (1) user embedding — online + offline store (online for inference lookup, offline for training), (2) user recent activity — online store with streaming ingestion via Kinesis/Lambda, offline store, (3) product catalog — offline store only (updates weekly, batch-lookup acceptable), or online if low-latency fetch is needed, (4) real-time session signals — computed per-request and passed directly in the inference payload, NOT in Feature Store (Feature Store isn't ideal for per-request computed values).
Store all features in DynamoDB directly, skip Feature Store.
Store all features in offline store only and read from S3 during inference.
questions.explanation:
The nuanced answer: Feature Store is great for features that are precomputed and fetched by key, but NOT ideal for per-request computed values. Real-time session signals (option B's item 4) are computed from the current request — there's nothing to 'look up' because the feature value depends on the current request context. Passing these in the inference payload is the right pattern.

Option A puts everything in online store, which is expensive for features that don't need low-latency fetch. Option C loses Feature Store's benefits (training-serving consistency, lineage). Option D makes inference too slow (S3 latency).
questions.pattern: Feature Store design: decide online vs offline storage per feature group based on inference latency needs. Real-time/session features that are computed per-request don't belong in Feature Store — pass them inline. Training-only features can be offline-only to save cost. The exam tests whether you think per-feature-group, not blanket decisions.

questions.scenario: A team deploys a customer support intent classifier with 95% accuracy in offline evaluation. In production, accuracy appears strong initially but users complain the model often misclassifies requests. Investigation shows: model predictions are technically correct by the training labels, but the training labels themselves were inconsistent (different labelers labeled similar tickets differently). The team wants to improve labeling quality without re-labeling 50,000 tickets from scratch.
questions.question: Which approach BEST addresses the label quality issue cost-effectively?
choices: Relabel all 50,000 tickets using a stricter labeling guide.

Use SageMaker Ground Truth with multi-labeler consensus (e.g., 3 labelers per ticket, agreement required) on a strategic subset: (1) tickets where the model is uncertain (low-confidence predictions), (2) tickets in categories with highest disagreement historically. Retrain on the relabeled subset combined with the original high-confidence-labeled data. Consider active learning to keep iterating on the hardest cases.
Use confidence scores to discard any training examples with model uncertainty > 20%.
Deploy a larger model that might learn to average out label noise.
questions.explanation:
Option B is the cost-effective targeted approach: re-label only where it matters (confusing categories, uncertain predictions) using multi-labeler consensus to get higher-quality labels, then retrain. This is active learning applied to label quality.

Option A is expensive and ignores that most labels may be fine. Option C throws away potentially-good data based on a noisy signal. Option D doesn't fix systematic label noise — larger models memorize noise harder.
questions.pattern: Label quality issues: don't blanket-relabel. Use active learning to identify high-value re-labeling candidates (uncertain predictions, high-disagreement categories). Multi-labeler consensus in Ground Truth. Iterative improvement. The exam tests whether you think strategically about labeling budget rather than throwing money at relabeling.

questions.scenario: An ML engineer is fine-tuning a large foundation model (7B parameters) on domain-specific data. They have 8 A100 GPUs available on a single ml.p4d.24xlarge instance. Training is 3x slower than expected, with frequent GPU utilization drops visible in nvidia-smi. No OOM errors. The training uses PyTorch DDP (DistributedDataParallel) with batch size 16 per GPU.
questions.question: Which is the MOST LIKELY bottleneck and fix?
choices: GPUs are memory-bound; reduce batch size to 8 per GPU.

Data loading is the bottleneck (CPU-bound preprocessing can't feed 8 A100s fast enough, causing GPUs to idle). Increase DataLoader num_workers, enable pin_memory, pre-tokenize/preprocess data offline, and consider using SageMaker FastFile or FSx for Lustre for faster data access. Validate with GPU utilization profiling using SageMaker Debugger.
DDP is suboptimal for 8 GPUs; switch to ZeRO-3 optimizer sharding.
The model is too large; switch to a 3B parameter model.
questions.explanation:
GPU utilization drops with no OOM errors is the signature of a data-loading bottleneck — GPUs are waiting for the next batch. The fix is not in the GPU/model config but in the data pipeline: more DataLoader workers, pinned memory, pre-tokenization, faster storage.

Option A (reduce batch size) makes it worse — less compute per batch means GPU spends more time idle between batches. Option C (ZeRO-3) addresses memory, not throughput. Option D (smaller model) avoids the problem instead of solving it.
questions.pattern: GPU training bottleneck diagnosis: GPU utilization consistently low = data loading issue. GPU utilization high but slow training = compute bound. OOM errors = memory bound. No OOM + low util = data pipeline. The fix toolkit: num_workers, pin_memory, prefetching, faster storage (FSx Lustre, FastFile mode), pre-processing offline.

questions.scenario: A model deployed in production is subject to regulatory oversight. Auditors demand: (1) ability to reproduce any historical prediction given the input, (2) proof that the model used for a specific prediction was the approved version, (3) ability to show what features contributed to any specific prediction (even ones from 6 months ago), (4) the raw input that triggered each prediction. Storage budget is reasonable but not unlimited.
