questions.scenario: A team uses SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning (AMT) with Bayesian strategy. Their budget is 200 training runs over 2 days. Each training run takes 45 minutes. They notice AMT is slow to converge — the best validation score after 50 runs is barely better than after 20 runs. They're considering switching strategies.
questions.question: Which hyperparameter tuning approach MOST likely finds better results faster?
choices: Switch to random search — Bayesian is over-hyped.

Switch to Hyperband — a multi-fidelity strategy that stops poorly-performing configurations early, enabling more configurations to be tried within the same budget. Especially effective for hyperparameters where early training performance correlates with final performance (common for neural nets, less so for small tabular models).
Add more training runs to the budget (500 instead of 200).
Use Grid Search with coarser granularity.
questions.explanation:
Hyperband is a multi-fidelity tuning strategy: start many configurations with small training budgets; stop poorly-performing ones early; give more budget to promising configurations. Effectively explores more configurations within a fixed total compute budget.

SageMaker AMT supports Hyperband. It's particularly effective when:
- Early training performance is a decent predictor of final performance (usually true for deep learning)
- Training has clear early/late phases (can stop at iteration N if loss is still bad)

Option A (random): not inherently better than Bayesian; worse on average for well-behaved spaces.

Option C (more budget): works but is linear in cost. Hyperband's efficiency gain can effectively give you more information per compute dollar.

Option D (grid): systematic but inefficient; Bayesian already better than grid for 7+ dimensions.

The subtle exam value: Hyperband is a less-known AMT strategy that specifically addresses 'Bayesian is slow to converge.' Exam may test whether you know the 4 AMT strategies (Grid, Random, Bayesian, Hyperband) and when each wins.

When Hyperband doesn't help: if training is very short (can't stop early meaningfully), or if early performance poorly predicts final (common for small tabular models with few epochs).
questions.pattern: AMT tuning strategies: Grid (exhaustive, small spaces), Random (baseline), Bayesian (efficient for expensive trials), Hyperband (multi-fidelity, early-stopping, best for deep learning with meaningful early signal). Hyperband when training has stop-early potential; Bayesian otherwise.

questions.scenario: A team builds a RAG application using Amazon Bedrock. They want to use a proprietary 5 GB document corpus. Retrieval quality is critical. They're deciding between Bedrock Knowledge Bases (managed RAG) vs. a custom vector store (e.g., OpenSearch Serverless with Bedrock embeddings). The team is small and doesn't want to manage embedding pipelines or tune retrieval.
questions.question: Which approach BEST fits the team's constraints?
choices: Custom OpenSearch Serverless with Bedrock embeddings gives maximum control over retrieval tuning.

Bedrock Knowledge Bases — managed RAG that handles document ingestion, chunking, embedding (using Bedrock embedding models), vector store (supports OpenSearch Serverless, Aurora pgvector, Pinecone, Redis), retrieval, and integration with foundation models. Minimal operational burden. Trade-off: less customization vs. rolling your own.
Self-host a vector store on EC2 for maximum cost control.
Skip RAG and fine-tune a foundation model on the corpus instead.
questions.explanation:
Bedrock Knowledge Bases is the managed RAG offering: handles the full pipeline (ingestion from S3, chunking, embedding, vector store indexing, retrieval, response generation). Uses Bedrock for both embeddings and generation. The team just provides the document corpus and questions; no pipeline management.

Trade-offs:
- Less customization than rolling your own
- Limited to supported vector stores and embedding models
- But: drastically less work for a small team

Option A (custom OpenSearch): valid for teams wanting full control, but requires building and maintaining the pipeline. Scenario explicitly says 'team doesn't want to manage.'

Option C (self-host): worst ops burden.

Option D (fine-tune): doesn't give the document-grounded responses RAG provides. Fine-tuning embeds knowledge in weights; RAG retrieves fresh content. For document QA, RAG is usually better than fine-tuning.

The subtle exam value: Bedrock Knowledge Bases is a newer feature. Exam tests whether you know this specific capability vs. defaulting to 'build with Bedrock + custom vector store.'
questions.pattern: RAG on AWS: Bedrock Knowledge Bases (managed, fast setup, less control) vs. custom (Bedrock embeddings + your vector store of choice, full control, more work). Small teams: Knowledge Bases. Advanced retrieval needs: custom. Don't fine-tune for document QA — use RAG.

questions.scenario: A team's training data ingestion pipeline reads from Kinesis Data Streams (real-time events), enriches with data from an RDS lookup table, and writes to S3 as Parquet. Current throughput is 2000 events/sec. They want to scale to 20000 events/sec without rewriting the pipeline logic.
questions.question: Which architecture BEST scales the enrichment + write?
choices: Replace Kinesis with SQS to decouple producer from consumer.

Use Kinesis Data Firehose with Lambda enrichment: Firehose handles parallelism and batching to S3 (including Parquet conversion), Lambda enrichment is invoked per batch not per record, and Kinesis enhanced fan-out can be used for other consumers. For RDS lookup, cache frequently-accessed rows in ElastiCache to reduce RDS load under high throughput.
Write custom code on EC2 for the pipeline.
Use AWS Glue streaming ETL jobs to replace the Kinesis-Lambda-S3 flow.
questions.explanation:
At 20000 events/sec, key bottlenecks:

- **Per-record Lambda invocation cost**: expensive. Firehose batches before invoking Lambda for transformation/enrichment, reducing invocation count.
- **RDS lookup under load**: 20k RDS queries/sec will overwhelm most RDS instances. ElastiCache caches the lookup table (if small and slow-changing), providing microsecond lookups.
- **S3 write parallelism**: Firehose handles batching and Parquet conversion natively.

Option A (SQS): SQS is decoupling, not throughput scaling; doesn't inherently solve the problem.

Option C (EC2 custom): more operational burden, solves same problem.

Option D (Glue streaming): valid alternative, but Firehose is more cost-effective for simple transform-and-load patterns. Glue streaming is better when complex windowed logic is needed.

The subtle exam value: Firehose + Lambda batch transformation is the AWS-canonical 'streaming ETL with transforms' pattern. Recognizing ElastiCache as the 'RDS lookup at scale' fix is another layer.
questions.pattern: Streaming ETL at scale: Kinesis Data Streams → Firehose (with Lambda batch transform + Parquet conversion) → S3. Enrichment lookups → cache in ElastiCache/DAX. Don't call RDS at 20k/sec.

questions.scenario: A team's ML dataset has a time-based structure: rows are transactions with timestamps. They want to split into train/validation/test. A junior engineer proposes random 70/15/15 split. A senior engineer objects.
questions.question: What is the CORRECT objection and approach?
choices: Random split is fine; the objection is unfounded.

For time-series data, random split causes temporal leakage — training data includes 'future' transactions relative to test data. Use temporal split: sort by time, use earliest 70% for training, middle 15% for validation, latest 15% for test. This simulates real prediction scenarios where the model predicts unseen future from observed past.
Use stratified split on the target variable instead.
Split by customer ID to avoid temporal leakage.
questions.explanation:
Temporal leakage is a common ML-on-time-data mistake. If rows are ordered in time and you randomly split, 'training' data contains transactions AFTER the test period from different customers. The model sees 'future' patterns (e.g., post-event behavior) when training, leading to inflated metrics that don't translate to production.

For time-series or any time-ordered data:
- Sort by time first
- Split temporally: earliest for train, then validation, then test
- This mirrors real-world usage where you train on past, predict future

Option A: misses the temporal leakage concern.

Option C (stratified on target): doesn't address temporal leakage.

Option D (split by customer ID): does help with customer-leakage (a different concern, where the same customer appears in both train and test), but doesn't address temporal leakage. Best practice combines both: temporal split AND customer-level awareness if customers span time periods.

The subtle exam value: temporal leakage is a specific failure mode that the exam tests. Distinct from customer-level data leakage. Both can apply; this scenario foregrounds temporal.
questions.pattern: Data splitting for time-series: temporal split, NOT random. Earliest-for-train, latest-for-test. Additional concern: customer/group leakage — same entity in multiple splits. For both, split by time AND group (e.g., group-aware time split). Random split on time-ordered data inflates metrics.

questions.scenario: A team is training a model with a dataset containing numerical features with vastly different scales: 'age' (0-100), 'annual_income' ($0-$1M), 'days_since_last_purchase' (0-3650). They're using an XGBoost model. A colleague suggests they need to normalize/standardize features.
questions.question: Which statement about feature scaling is CORRECT for this XGBoost case?
choices: XGBoost (like other tree-based models) is invariant to feature scaling because trees split on thresholds, not distances. Scaling doesn't change splits. For tree-based models, scaling is generally unnecessary — don't add unnecessary preprocessing steps. For distance-based models (KNN, SVM, neural nets, K-means), scaling IS necessary.
Always scale features regardless of model type.
Normalize using min-max to [0,1] for all features.
Standardize to zero mean, unit variance for all features.
questions.explanation:
This tests ML algorithm understanding. Tree-based models (decision trees, random forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) are invariant to monotonic transformations of features — the splits at 'age > 40' and 'age/100 > 0.4' produce identical trees. Scaling doesn't help.

Distance/gradient-based models DO need scaling:
- KNN, SVM, K-means: distance computations dominated by large-scale features
- Linear models with regularization: coefficient magnitudes depend on feature scales
- Neural networks: gradient updates sensitive to feature scales; poorly-scaled features cause training instability

Wrong answers (B, C, D) all assume 'always scale.' Extra preprocessing adds complexity without benefit for tree models and can introduce bugs (e.g., train-time scaler not applied at inference).

The subtle exam value: the exam tests whether you know which preprocessing is required per algorithm. Over-scaling tree models is a common junior mistake; under-scaling NNs is another common mistake.
questions.pattern: Feature scaling by algorithm: Tree-based (XGBoost, RF) = not needed. Distance-based (KNN, K-means, SVM) = required. Gradient-based (NN, linear+regularization) = required. Apply scaling only when it helps; don't add unnecessary preprocessing.

questions.scenario: A team's production ML model makes recommendations. A data science intern notices the model recommends different products to users based on zip code, and zip code correlates with protected demographic attributes. The team is concerned about proxy discrimination. They want to audit whether removing zip code from features would fix the issue.
questions.question: Which analysis MOST rigorously answers 'is zip code a proxy for protected attributes'?
choices: Remove zip code and see if predictions change for different demographic groups.

Use SageMaker Clarify to compute post-training bias metrics across protected attributes (age, race, gender as available), with and without zip code as a feature. Compare feature attribution: if zip code has high SHAP importance AND the disparate impact metric changes substantially when zip code is removed, zip code is acting as a proxy. Also check if remaining features together act as a combined proxy.
Trust that removing zip code is sufficient.
Only use demographic-parity fairness metrics.
questions.explanation:
This is a rigorous fairness audit. Proxy discrimination analysis requires multiple steps:

1. **Compute bias metrics across protected groups** (Clarify post-training bias analysis): disparate impact, statistical parity, equalized odds, etc. Do current predictions differ by protected attribute?

2. **Compute feature attribution** (SHAP via Clarify): which features drive predictions most? If zip code is top-5 for protected-group predictions specifically, it's influential.

3. **Counterfactual testing**: train with and without zip code; does bias metric change? If yes, zip code is contributing to bias.

4. **Check combined proxies**: other features (shopping history, browse patterns) may together act as proxies even without zip code. Removing one doesn't mean removing proxy effects.

Option A (just remove and observe): incomplete — doesn't quantify, doesn't check combined proxies.

Option C (just remove): naive.

Option D (single fairness metric): different fairness definitions (demographic parity, equalized odds, etc.) can conflict; multiple metrics give fuller picture.

The subtle exam value: this tests whether you know fairness analysis is multi-step, not 'check one metric.' Clarify supports multiple bias metrics and feature attribution in one workflow.
questions.pattern: ML fairness audit: compute multiple bias metrics (Clarify post-training bias) + feature attribution (SHAP via Clarify) + counterfactual (with/without suspect feature) + check combined proxies. Single-metric or single-feature analyses are insufficient for rigorous audits.

references.title: Fairness in ML with SageMaker Clarify

questions.scenario: A team has 500,000 images for a multi-class image classification task (50 classes). A colleague recommends training a ResNet-50 from scratch for 'maximum control.' The team has 4x ml.p4d.24xlarge instances and wants results within 2 weeks. Training from scratch shows validation accuracy plateauing at 68% after 100 epochs.
questions.question: Which approach MOST LIKELY produces the best accuracy within constraints?
choices: Continue training from scratch for more epochs.

Use transfer learning from a pre-trained ResNet-50 (ImageNet-trained, available via SageMaker JumpStart or PyTorch Hub): replace the final classification layer with 50 outputs, fine-tune on the team's data. This typically reaches much higher accuracy than from-scratch training with this dataset size. For 500k images, either fine-tune the whole network or fine-tune last layers only — empirical test will tell which.
Increase model capacity to ResNet-152.
Reduce the number of classes by merging similar ones.
questions.explanation:
500,000 images sounds like a lot, but for a 23-million-parameter ResNet-50, it's borderline. ImageNet (used for pre-training) has 14 million images across 1,000 classes. Pre-trained weights encode vastly more visual pattern information than 500k from-scratch training can learn.

Transfer learning from ImageNet:
- Replace final layer (1000 outputs → 50 outputs)
- Fine-tune: either all layers (typical for 500k images) or just top layers (for smaller datasets)
- Usually reaches 85-95%+ accuracy when task is similar to ImageNet domain

SageMaker JumpStart provides pre-trained image models out of the box for fine-tuning.

Option A (more epochs from scratch): diminishing returns; 68% plateau suggests model isn't learning adequate features from the data alone.

Option C (larger model): more parameters with the same data will overfit worse.

Option D (merge classes): avoids the problem rather than solving it.

The subtle exam value: this tests whether you recognize 'train from scratch' as almost-always-wrong for image classification with limited data. Transfer learning is the near-universal right answer for image tasks, with 'how much to fine-tune' being the subsidiary question.
questions.pattern: Image classification with <10M images → transfer learning from ImageNet (or domain-specific pre-trained model). From-scratch ResNet training is almost always wrong unless you have ImageNet-scale data. SageMaker JumpStart provides ready-to-fine-tune image models. Similar logic for NLP: pre-trained transformers > from-scratch.
